title: Keith Thomas' Religion and the Decline of Magic
blurb: Thomas's landmark social history explained how and why early-modern England stopped believing in magic — the founding work of the field this project draws on.
source: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (renaissance magic corpus)

title: Dungeons & Dragons invents the game wizard
blurb: Gygax and Arneson's D&D turned the magical vocabulary into rules — spell slots, the wizard and cleric classes, and the lich whose soul hides in a phylactery, wrenching that word to a new meaning.
mtg: The rulebook grammar of magic that MTG would inherit and refine.

title: Kieckhefer's Magic in the Middle Ages
blurb: Richard Kieckhefer's synthesis made medieval magic teachable — mapping the 'common tradition' and the clerical underworld of necromancy that our histories keep citing.
source: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages (medieval magic corpus)

title: Magic: The Gathering — Alpha
blurb: Richard Garfield's trading-card game shipped, and with it a corpus that would grow to tens of thousands of cards — the largest published fantasy vocabulary of magic ever assembled, and this study's raw material.
mtg: Year zero of the corpus: 34,629 unique cards and counting.

title: Harry Potter mainstreams the wizard
blurb: Rowling's series put wizards, spells, and a magic school at the centre of global popular culture, cementing the fantasy sense of nearly every word in this lexicon.

title: Owen Davies, Grimoires: A History
blurb: The first full scholarly history of the grimoire traced the spell-book from the ancient world to the internet — the book about the books of magic.

title: Scry becomes a Magic keyword
blurb: The set Fifth Dawn introduced Scry — look at the top cards of your library and reorder them — bolting a precise new meaning onto a four-century-old word for crystal-gazing.
mtg: A divination word made a mechanic inside living memory; see the Word vs Keyword chart.

title: Theros: Magic goes Greek
blurb: The Theros set drew on Greek myth — oracles, sibylline prophecy, gods and the underworld — and made Scry an evergreen mechanic, folding ancient divination into the game's spine.
mtg: The game reaching back to the very sources this timeline begins with.

title: Eldritch Moon and the Eldrazi
blurb: MTG's Innistrad horror block climaxed with Lovecraftian world-eaters, carrying 'eldritch' — via Lovecraft, via a Scots word for the otherworldly — onto the cards.

title: The Routledge History of Medieval Magic
blurb: Sophie Page and Catherine Rider's volume gathered the state of the field — the scholarly apparatus behind many of this site's Word Histories.
source: Page & Rider (eds.), The Routledge History of Medieval Magic (medieval magic corpus)

title: Ward becomes a keyword
blurb: Zendikar Rising made Ward an evergreen protective ability — the oldest idea of protective magic, the Old English weardian, formalised as a rules keyword.

title: Strixhaven: a whole university of mages
blurb: Strixhaven built a set around five colleges of magic, each a colour-pair, turning the study of magic itself — the Persian magus's distant legacy — into the setting.

title: Alchemy and the Conjure keyword
blurb: MTG Arena's digital-only Alchemy format introduced the keyword action Conjure — create a named card out of nothing — reversing the word's old sense of binding a spirit by oath into pure creation.
mtg: The etymology turned inside out: from contract to creation.

title: Wilds of Eldraine: fairy-tale enchantment
blurb: A storybook set of curses, bargains, and glamour — the folk-magic end of the vocabulary, where enchantment still means what it did in the ballads.

title: The Vocabulary of Magic (this project)
blurb: This study mines the full MTG corpus for the words of magic, traces each to its historical root through the medieval- and renaissance-magic databases, and lays the whole genealogy out — the timeline you are reading.
mtg: Where the game's vocabulary meets its own five-thousand-year history.
